q: How many Avari stayed behind at Cuiviénen?
a: The Nature of Middle-earth DOES count them, repeatedly and with different results — 5,000 remaining against 10,000 marching in one scheme; 8,748 Avari against 17,496 Eldar in another; 16,056 and 9,000 elsewhere. What is missing is not a number but a settled one, and any count of the Avari in later Ages.
r: The demographic model carries them as a stated blank; no eastern census exists. — Corrected 2026-07-24: the earlier entry claimed the corpus 'never counts them'. It counts them several times over, in figures Tolkien did not reconcile.

q: What are the populations of Harad, Rhûn and Khand?
a: Armies beyond counting are attested — hosts that outnumber the West ten times at the Morannon — but never a census, a city-count, or a homeland survey.
r: The armies model derives lower bounds from hosts and refuses totals.

q: How do Orcs reproduce — and are there female Orcs?
a: HoME X says they 'had life and multiplied after the manner of the Children of Ilúvatar'; no female orc, family, or lifespan is anywhere described.
r: Orc numbers in the archive are army-derived only, with the gap flagged on every chart.

q: How large were the hosts of the War of Wrath?
a: The corpus gives outcomes — Thangorodrim broken, Beleriand drowned, the dragon-fleet's last sortie — but not one number for either side.
r: The battle stands in the armies hall as a dashed, unnumbered marker with the exact refusal.

q: What strength assailed Lórien and Mirkwood in 3019?
a: App B records three assaults on Lórien and the long battle under the trees — outcomes only, no figures.
r: Two dashed markers in the armies hall carry the refusal verbatim.

a: Three cited exceptions exist — the nissi in desperate defence, Haleth's guard, Éowyn — and no counts for any of them.
r: The armies model reports female share ≈ 0 with exceptions named, rather than inventing percentages.

a: The last son of Fëanor cast his Silmaril into the sea; the Silmarillion leaves him wandering the shores in lament — and never ends his story.
r: His genealogy card reads 'unknown (wanders)' — the archive's only open-ended Fëanorian.

a: Tolkien answered this twice, and differently. In one letter: they 'had disappeared for good, being destroyed with their gardens in the War of the Last Alliance… when Sauron pursued a scorched earth policy and burned their land'. In another he withdraws it: 'As for the Entwives: I do not know.' The narrative itself keeps only the lost gardens and Treebeard's grief.
r: The Ents' population line runs to extinction with fertility zero — and the question stays a question. — Corrected 2026-07-24: the archive previously showed only the narrative silence and neither of Tolkien's own answers.

a: Within the tale he is deliberately unexplained — Tolkien confirms the opacity is intentional. Outside it he does gloss him: Bombadil is 'an “allegory”, or an exemplar, a particular embodying of pure (real) natural science: the spirit that desires knowledge of other things, their history and nature, because they are “other” and wholly independent of the enquiring mind'.
r: He appears nowhere in the archive's trees, peoples or armies — deliberately. — Corrected 2026-07-24: what the corpus withholds is a taxonomy, not an account. Letter 153 gives the account.

a: After Sam's blade and the Phial, the text says only that this tale does not record it.
r: Her lair is pinned on the map; her end is not.

q: What was the mithril-coat's final road?
a: last attested worn at the Scouring of the Shire, 3 Nov 3019 — and then the corpus falls silent; over Sea or left behind, no text says.
r: The artifact chain ends with the gap declared, not bridged.

a: She sailed south from the wreck of the realm with the youngest son — the corpus never names her.
r: The family trees carry the marriage as an unnamed node would lie — they simply do not draw what is not given.

a: App A counts five children of Thengel and names two; three daughters go unnamed forever.
r: The genealogy notes the count and draws only the named.

a: She is named once. In the War of the Jewels variant where Gil-galad is Orodreth's son, Orodreth 'sent away his wife MERIL to her own folk in Eglorest, and with her went their son, yet an elvenchild, and Gilgalad Starlight he was called'. The late Peoples of Middle-earth text keeps her unnamed: 'Their mother was a Sindarin lady of the North. She called her son Gil-galad.' Under the published descent from Fingon, no mother is named at all.
r: The trees show all three paternal variants; the maternal line is a blank the archive refuses to fill. — Corrected 2026-07-24: the archive previously asserted that no version ever names a mother. One does, in the very variant it cited.

a: A grandmother 'stern and wise' rules the clan by the Gladden; between her and him, no parent is named, and Déagol's kinship is left as 'friend' and kin unspecified.
r: Both stand as honest dashed links in the trees.

q: How did Narsil come from Telchar's forge to Elendil's hand?
a: First Age Nogrod to Second Age Númenor — an heirloom road of thousands of years with not one recorded holder between.
r: The artifact chain marks it 'honest gap' in so many words.

a: The corpus places it, though it cannot be reached: 'far off in the east of Middle-earth, and northward, and it was a bay in the Inland Sea of Helcar'. The Ambarkanta sets it 'to the North beside the waters of Helkar' and Christopher notes it is shown on map IV. NoME even measures it — 2,000 miles as the crow flies from the coast of ancient Beleriand.
r: The living map ends where the corpus's geography ends; Cuiviénen is cited, not pinned. — Corrected 2026-07-24: the location is given in the same sentence the archive had quoted to prove it unknown. What is truly lost is the land itself, drowned in the changing of the world.

q: What lies in the far East and South?
a: Client-kingdoms, two Blue Wizards, and rumor. No map, no census, no history of Khand or the Sunlands exists in the corpus.
r: The realm-slices mark Rhûn 'schematic'; nothing further is drawn.

q: What did Númenor look like, city by city?
a: UT gives one map-description chapter and a handful of places; the island drowned with its geography mostly unwritten.
r: The archive renders Númenor as data (population, fleets, kings) — not as a mapped land.
